case_name: James R. Snell v. Behavioral Health Connections, Inc. UHS of Timberlawn, Inc., Universal Health Services, Inc., and Heather Cawthon

summary.brief: James R. Snell v. Behavioral Health Connections, Inc. UHS of Timberlawn, Inc., Universal Health Services, Inc., and Heather Cawthon. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: Snell claims during the seventeen-day period between those two dates, he was illegally held and effectively 'imprisoned' at the facility.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Snell was humiliated and ridiculed, particularly for protesting that he was being falsely imprisoned.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm the trial court’s take-nothing judgment in this case.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The trial court did not err in dismissing Snell’s claims against appellees for his failure to serve an expert report under the TMLA.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Whether a pleading asserts a health care liability claim presents a question of law courts review de novo.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Snell first met Cawthon, a licensed professional counselor.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Snell’s amended petition identified Cawthon as a licensed professional counselor and BHC employee.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court granted Timberlawn’s motion to dismiss.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Snell was physically restrained and forcibly medicated on two of those dates.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We are mindful of the serious and important liberty interests at stake in this and similar cases.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Early on the morning of December 9, 2018, the patient became verbally active, seeming disoriented and confused while a pedestrian on a residential street in his neighborhood, possibly due to side effects from a prescription anti-inflammatory medication. Someone contacted police who called paramedics, and who, in turn, gave him a drug that put him to sleep and took him to the emergency room at Baylor Hospital in Dallas.

reveal.result_summary: Patient evaluated by Heather Cawthon, LPC, Ph.D.

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

key_events.event: Patient brought to ED by paramedics
Initial mental health evaluation by Cawthon

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in emergency room with confusion and disorientation
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Conduct a thorough medical evaluation to rule out non-psychiatric causes
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to rule out medical causes before psychiatric commitment
Premature without ruling out medical causes

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant proceeded with involuntary psychiatric commitment
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed patient was a danger to himself or others

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for medical evaluation

patient_outcome.description: Patient was involuntarily committed for 17 days, physically restrained and forcibly medicated on two occasions.
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Patient reported lasting psychological impact and reduced quality of life.

key_findings: Court found no breach of duty as claims were dismissed for failure to serve expert report

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to conduct thorough medical evaluation before psychiatric commitment
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: A thorough medical evaluation could have identified non-psychiatric causes

factual_timeline.event: Patient became disoriented and confused, taken to ED

allegations.against: Behavioral Health Connections, Inc., UHS of Timberlawn, Inc., Universal Health Services, Inc., and Heather Cawthon

court_findings.finding: No breach of duty as claims were dismissed for failure to serve expert report
